---
title: Open a course repository
---

Before editing a new repository, identify how it is built and where its
maintained source documents live. Course repositories share broad roles, but
not necessarily the same directory names or task arguments.

## First inspection

Look for these files and directories:

| Location | What it tells you |
| --- | --- |
| `README.md` or an authoring guide | The intended contributor workflow and supported commands |
| `pixi.toml` or `pyproject.toml` | Dependencies and tasks such as `preview` or `render` |
| `_quarto.yml` | Project type, output formats, navigation, and resources |
| `_quarto-<profile>.yml` | Optional learner-facing or answer-bearing variants |
| `*.qmd` content folders | The source documents to edit |
| `_extensions/` or `filters/` | Installed features such as `quarto-teaching-tools` solution handling |
| `.github/workflows/` | Automated render and publication behavior |
| `_site/`, `_output/`, or similar | Rendered output; usually inspect, do not edit |

## Establish the workflow

From the repository root:

1. Read its documentation and task manifest.
2. Identify the `.qmd` file associated with the page or exercise to change.
3. Determine whether there are multiple profiles or output formats to verify.
4. Run the documented preview task before editing.
5. Make the smallest source change necessary and re-check its rendered output.

::: {.callout-tip title="Patterns seen in existing courses"}
An ordinary website may contain `course_notes/_quarto.yml` and use
`pixi run preview`. A new profiled project should prefer
`pixi run preview student` and `pixi run preview instructor`, backed by
`teaching.show-solutions`. Existing projects may instead use names such as
`exercise`/`solution` or `student`/`master`; their configured command remains
authoritative.
:::
